text: The selective alkene epoxidation remains a highly energy demanding and selective process in catalytic production. Here, we introduce a photoelectrochemical (PEC) process capable of producing various types of epoxides in very high selectivities and faradaic efficiencies. Employing an $\alpha$-Fe$_2$O$_3$ photoanode and a Br$^-$/BrO$^-$ solution redox couple allowed us to achieve highly selective and selective water-based PEC oxidation of alkenes (selectivities, $>$99\% and Faradaic efficiencies up to 82 $\pm$ 4\%). These values surpass those of the direct electrochemical and direct PEC oxidation of alkenes (electro- and photo-oxidation). A detailed analysis of the reaction pathway via isotopic experiments, Hammett analysis and investigations of the photocatalytic activity of TiO$_2$ as well as the performance of a platinum electrode revealed that the oxidation of bromide ions into hypobromite ions occurs at the hematite surface via an oxygen atom transfer (OAT) reaction from iron-oxo species of high valency. These ions lead to the selective oxygen-atom transfer reaction in the solution in which the formed hypobromite ion catalytically brings about the alkene oxidation. The reaction mechanism does not involve any radical species or the high overpotentials that are known to occur in the direct oxidation reactions. Therefore, the novel PEC process based on the hematite photoanode bears great promise to become a competitive technology in catalytic production for making selectively and catalytically valuable epoxides using solar energy. The produced hydrogen can be easily harvested in the H$_2$ chamber of the solar photoanodic fuel cell.
explanation: The passage is highly technical with concrete data, consistent with human-authored scientific text, but similar levels of detail can be produced by AI; overall, a moderate likelihood of AI generation.
